s: Aardvark teeth have no enamel and grow continuously throughout their lives — and each tooth contains up to 1,500 tiny hexagonal tubes, making their teeth unlike any other mammal's on Earth.
q: What makes aardvark teeth unique among all mammals?
a: They have no enamel and contain tiny hexagonal tubes
d: They have extremely hard enamel that never wears down
They are replaced throughout life like shark teeth
They are fused together in solid blocks
They contain calcium deposits for strength
They are hollow like bird bones
They are composed entirely of dentin without root systems
e: Aardvark means 'earth pig' in Afrikaans, but they are not related to pigs at all. Their tubular teeth (which inspired the scientific order name Tubulidentata) are composed of dentine columns cemented together, creating a unique honeycomb-like cross-section. Aardvarks can eat up to 50,000 termites in a single night using a 30 cm sticky tongue. They are the only living member of their entire order — as taxonomically unique as humans are from mice.

s: African grey parrots can learn over 1,000 words and use them contextually, with cognitive abilities comparable to a 5-year-old human child.
q: What historic question did Alex the African grey parrot famously ask?
d: Why do parrots live so long?
e: Dr. Irene Pepperberg's research with an African grey named Alex demonstrated these parrots understand concepts of zero, color, shape, same/different, and can combine words creatively. Alex famously asked 'What color am I?' — possibly the first question asked by a non-human animal.

s: African lungfish can survive without water for up to 4 years by burrowing into mud, secreting a mucus cocoon around themselves, and entering a dormant state called aestivation.
q: How long can African lungfish survive without water?
a: Up to 4 years through aestivation in a mucus cocoon
d: Up to 2 years by slowing metabolism
Up to 1 year in protective spores
Up to 8 years in mud burrows
Only a few weeks in dormancy
e: During drought, lungfish dig into drying mud, curl up, and encase themselves in dried mucus that hardens into a protective cocoon. Their metabolism drops to nearly zero, and they breathe air through a small tube to the surface. When rains return years later, they revive within hours.

s: African marsh harriers hunt by flying incredibly slowly over reedbeds — sometimes nearly hovering — with their long legs dangling below ready to snatch frogs and rodents the moment they detect movement.
q: How do African marsh harriers catch prey in dense reedbeds?
a: They fly very slowly with legs dangling, ready to grab prey on detection
d: They dive from high altitude at extreme speeds
They hunt using echolocation like owls
They use their wings to flush prey from reeds
They wade through shallow water on long legs
They hover motionless to surprise prey
e: Circus ranivorus (whose species name means 'frog-eater') specializes in hunting over wetlands. Unlike most raptors that dive from height, marsh harriers quarter low and slow over marshland, using their exceptional hearing to detect prey rustling through reeds. Their facial disk — unusual for a raptor — works like an owl's to funnel sound. They have the longest legs relative to body size of almost any raptor, perfect for reaching down into dense vegetation.

s: African skimmers fly with their elongated lower beak slicing through the water surface — when it hits a fish, the jaw snaps shut in 0.02 seconds, one of the fastest reflexes in the animal kingdom.
q: What is unique about the African skimmer's beak compared to all other birds?
a: Its lower mandible is longer than the upper one
d: Its upper mandible is longer than the lower one
It has two separate beaks for filtering food
It has a serrated edge for cutting prey
Its beak folds in half when closed
It has a whistle-like cavity for echolocation
e: The skimmer is the only bird whose lower mandible is significantly longer than the upper one. They fly just above the water at dusk and dawn, dragging their knife-like lower bill through the surface. The tactile snap reflex — triggered by touch, not sight — is so fast it works even in total darkness. Their pupils narrow to vertical slits, unique among birds, to reduce glare from the water.

s: African wild dogs vote on whether to go hunting by sneezing — when enough pack members sneeze, the group decides to move. Dominant dogs need fewer sneezes to trigger a hunt.
q: How do African wild dogs decide to start a hunt?
a: By sneezing — enough sneezes triggers a group hunt
d: By howling in unison until most agree
The dominant male always decides alone
By gathering in a circle and pushing
By raising their tails in patterns
The eldest female makes all decisions
e: Researchers at Botswana's Moremi Game Reserve observed that wild dogs use audible sneezes as a quorum-based voting mechanism before rallies (group hunts). When a dominant individual sneezed, only 3 sneezes were needed; for subordinates, the threshold was about 10 sneezes.

s: Alpine choughs have been observed flying at 8,200 metres on Mount Everest — the highest confirmed altitude for any bird — where oxygen levels are just one-third of sea level.
q: At what altitude have Alpine choughs been observed on Everest?
e: These crow relatives follow mountaineers, scavenging food scraps at extreme camps. Their blood carries oxygen more efficiently than lowland birds thanks to a haemoglobin variant that binds oxygen more tightly. They nest in cliff faces up to 6,500 metres, the highest nesting altitude of any bird.

s: Alpine ibex can climb near-vertical dam walls in Italy to lick mineral salts from the stone — walking on ledges just a few centimeters wide at heights that would terrify most rock climbers.
q: Why do Alpine ibex climb nearly vertical dam walls in Italy?
a: To lick mineral salts from the stone surface
d: To access nesting sites on cliff faces
e: The Cingino Dam in the Italian Alps is famous for photos of ibex casually walking up its nearly vertical face. Their cloven hooves have a hard outer edge for gripping and a soft inner pad that acts like a suction cup on smooth surfaces. They need the mineral salts (particularly sodium and calcium) that are absent from their alpine grass diet. Kids (baby ibex) can climb these walls within days of birth.

s: Alpine ibex regularly climb near-vertical dam walls in the Italian Alps to lick mineral-rich salts from the stone surface, defying gravity on slopes steeper than 80 degrees.
q: Why do Alpine ibex climb near-vertical dam walls?
a: To lick mineral-rich salts from the stone
d: To escape predators on the ground
To cool off in mist from the dam
e: The ibex's split hooves have soft, grippy pads that work like climbing shoes, and the two halves can spread independently to grip tiny ledges. They climb dam faces at the Cingino Dam in Piedmont, Italy, and have become a tourist attraction.
